retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Etiology -- Genetic Associations of Type 1 Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: The lifetime risk of developing T1D is significantly increased in close relatives of a patient with T1D. However, most cases occur in patients without any family history of T1D or other autoimmune disease. This association is more pronounced in youth-onset than adult-onset T1D. [7] Multiple other genes also contribute to heritability. [8] Screening of family members must be considered, especially first-degree relatives of individuals with T1D, to identify people who may be at risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Type 1 Diabetes -- Etiology -- Genetic Associations of Type 1 Diabetes
retrieved_docs.content: The exact etiology of T1D remains unknown. However, a genetic predisposition is strongly associated with specific human leukocyte antigen (HLA) alleles DR and DQ. HLA genes have been reported to account for approximately 40% of the familial aggregation of T1D. The HLA class II DRB1, -DQA1, -DQB1 genotypes confer the strongest genetic risk factors for T1D. [6] Specifically, HLA DR4-DQ8 and DR3-DQ2 have been reported to be present in about 90% of children with T1D.

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: There are now at least 14 different known MODY mutations. They include GCK, HNF1A, HNF4A, HNF1B, INS,  NEURO1, PDX1, PAX4, ABCC8, KCNJ11, KLF11, CEL, BLK, and APPL1 . The different genes vary with respect to the age of onset, response to treatment, and the presence of extra-pancreatic manifestations. The most common gene mutations are the following: Gene mutation in the hepatocyte nuclear factor 1 alpha ( HNF1A ) accounts for 30% to 60% of MODY. Gene mutation in the hepatocyte nuclear factor 4 alpha ( HNF4A ) accounts for 5% to 10% of MODY cases. Gene mutations in glucokinase ( GCK ) account for 30% to 60% of the cases of MODY. Gene mutation in hepatocyte nuclear factor 1 beta ( HNF1B ) accounts for less than 5% of the cases of MODY. The difference in the prevalence of the MODY genes varies from country to country, which may be, in part, due to differences in reporting.

retrieved_docs.title: Latent Autoimmune Diabetes -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The only genetic similarity with T2DM at the HNF1A locus was not reproduced in a subsequent study by the same investigators, who found a strong signal at the PFKFB3 locus during a landmark Genome-wide association study (GWAS). [29] LADA subtypes differ from GADA titers that are inversely related to BMI. [30] The inflammatory biomarkers associated with obesity vary when sera from various types of DM are analyzed. If these results are reproduced in larger populations, their utility to diagnose and differentiate LADA from T1DM and T2DM in conjunction with antibody testing may be enhanced. [31] Furthermore, a positive correlation exists in patients with LADA between BMI and interleukin-17, a pro-inflammatory cytokine from peripheral B lymphocytes implicated in T2DM and obesity. [32]

retrieved_docs.content: A defect in IPF1 (insulin gene promoter factor 1) causes another form of MODY. PDX1 is a homeobox-containing transcription factor that affects pancreatic development and insulin gene expression.

retrieved_docs.title: Primary Ovarian Insufficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Primary ovarian insufficiency is thought to arise from either follicular dysfunction or follicular depletion. However, the exact mechanisms in which POI develops remain unknown. [1] [3] Approximately 90% of all diagnosed cases of spontaneous POI do not have a determined underlying etiology. [6] Starting at the level of the ovary, rare mutations in the genes for FSH and LH receptors can alter the ovaries’ response to these circulating gonadotropins leading to nonfunctional ovarian tissue. [1] [2] [7] Iatrogenic causes of POI such as oophorectomy, chemotherapy, or radiation, as well as women with mumps, varicella, malaria, shigella, and TB can destroy healthy ovarian tissue and substantially decrease the amount of functioning tissue that remains. [1] [2] [3] [7] Chromosomal abnormalities such as Turner Syndrome (X,0) can lead to early oocyte apoptosis in utero, in addition to accelerated oocyte depletion early in life (before 10 years old). This leaves a woman with little to no ovarian reserve near reproductive age. [1] Fragile X FMR1 premutation (59-199 CGG trinucleotide repeats) significantly increases a woman’s risk of POI in comparison to the general population. Interestingly, women with FMR1 premutation of 80-100 CGG repeats are at the highest risk of POI. [1] Autoimmune diseases such as adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease), hypothyroidism (Hashimoto thyroiditis), rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, type 1 diabetes mellitus, nontypical congenital adrenal hyperplasia, myasthenia gravis, and inflammatory bowel disease have also been associated with POI. [1] [7] Genetic mutations and autosomal recessive diseases such as galactosemia (reduction of galactose-1-phosphate), ataxia-telangiectasia (ATM gene), and blepharophimosis-ptosis-epicanthus-inversus syndrome (known as BPES, a mutation in FOXL2) have undefined effects on POI. [1] [2] [7] Environmental exposures such as smoking, nicotine, and the various substances produced (dimethylbenzanthracene) are hypothesized to contribute to POI by binding to the receptors on ovarian granulosa cells. This leads to activation of proapoptotic genes and inhibits aromatase, ultimately leading to decreased circulating estradiol. [2] Other environmental exposures, such as substances in plastic production (phthalates, bisphenol-A), may contribute to POI, but the exact mechanism remains unknown. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Maturity Onset Diabetes in the Young -- Evaluation -- Who Should be Genetically Tested for MODY?
retrieved_docs.content: Molecular genetic testing helps to identify which mutation is involved. The type of testing performed depends on the clinician's pretest probability of knowing which particular gene is involved. Should a patient have particular characteristics of a specific subtype, such as typical associated extra-pancreatic features, single-gene testing or serial single-gene testing can be performed. If a patient's phenotype cannot be differentiated from other subtypes, one may opt to perform a MODY multigene panel which tests for 14 known genes to be associated with MODY. Finally, if the patient has clinically suggestive features, but the mutation cannot be found utilizing conventional testing, one can consider comprehensive genomic testing. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Diabetes -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The genetic background for both types is critical as a risk factor. As the human genome gets further explored, there are different loci found that confer risk for DM. Polymorphisms have been known to influence the risk for T1DM, including major histocompatibility complex (MHC) and human leukocyte antigen (HLA). [1]

retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the network of relationships portrays diabetes as a multifaceted condition that influences and is influenced by genetic abnormalities, neonatal presentations, limb and ocular complications, metabolic comorbidities, diagnostic testing, and medication safety, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 683
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes (مرض السكري) is presented as a central disease that is linked to a wide range of clinical findings, genetic factors, therapeutic considerations, and related disorders. Various forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (disorder), and the syndrome of Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to diabetes, highlighting its relevance from birth onward. Genetic dysfunction of the human INSR protein is specifically associated with diabetes, underscoring a molecular basis for the disease. Clinically, diabetes is associated with several severe complications of the lower extremities. Amputation of lower extremity includes child procedures such as toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), both of which are clinically associated with diabetes. Necrosis of the amputation stump of the right lower limb is an associated morphology (النَخَر) and is linked

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479
retrieved_docs.content: Disease condition determination, uncontrolled is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating that an uncontrolled disease state is linked to the presence of diabetes mellitus that has progressed to a complicated form, underscoring the importance of effective disease management to prevent or mitigate diabetic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2716
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 is identified as مرض السكري. مرض السكري is clinically associated with pancreatic disorders (not diabetes) and with اضطراب في الشبكية, indicating that patients with diabetes may also present with other pancreatic abnormalities and retinal disturbances. In addition, مرض السكري has rizikové faktory, highlighting the presence of specific risk factors that contribute to the development and progression of the disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1990
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and the combined condition Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to مرض السكري (diabetes), indicating that various forms of neonatal diabetes and associated neurodevelopmental disorders are classified under the broader disease of diabetes. The human INSR protein, when its function is impaired, is linked to the pathogenesis of مرض السكري, highlighting the role of insulin‑receptor signaling in the disease. Clinically, مرض السكري is associated with اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder), reflecting the common ocular complications of diabetes. Pharmacologically, the contraceptive agent desogestrel and the medication ethynodioldiacetát are contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, underscoring the need to avoid these drugs in diabetic care. Additionally, متلازمة الأيض (metabolic syndrome) is described as a child of مرض السكري, indicating a close interrelationship in which metabolic syndrome often coexists with or progresses to diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse clinical presentations, genetic factors, comorbidities, and therapeutic considerations relevant to diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2495
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is linked to a range of clinical manifestations and therapeutic considerations. Toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية) are clinically associated with diabetes, reflecting the disease’s potential to cause severe peripheral vascular and ocular complications. Specific forms of diabetes that occur in the neonatal period, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder)), are mapped to the broader category of diabetes, indicating that these transient neonatal presentations are recognized as variants of the disease. Additionally, the combination of developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes is also mapped to diabetes, highlighting a possible neurodevelopmental syndrome associated with early‑onset diabetes. From a pharmacologic perspective, the hormonal contraceptives desogestrel and norgestrel are contraindicated in individuals with diabetes, underscoring the need for careful medication selection in diabetic patients. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse systemic effects of diabetes, its neonatal forms, associated neurological conditions, and important medication contraindications that are essential for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1841
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1, and Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes are all mapped to the disease مرض السكري, indicating that these specific neonatal forms of diabetes and the syndrome that combines developmental delay, epilepsy, and neonatal diabetes are considered manifestations of diabetes. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting the well‑known link between diabetes and retinal disorders such as diabetic retinopathy. In addition, malfunction of the human INSR protein is gene‑product‑associated with مرض السكري, highlighting the role of insulin receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Finally, متلازمة الأيض is described as a child condition of مرض السكري, underscoring the close relationship between diabetes and metabolic syndrome in patient care and disease management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1506
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all mapped to مرض السكري, indicating that these distinct neonatal and genetic forms of diabetes are considered subtypes of diabetes mellitus. مرض السكري is clinically associated with اضطراب في الشبكية, reflecting a general retinal disorder that can occur in diabetic patients, and more specifically with اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ (diabetic retinopathy), a hallmark microvascular complication of diabetes. In addition, مرض السكري is clinically associated with مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية (end‑stage renal disease), demonstrating that chronic diabetes can progress to severe kidney failure. The relationship between اعتِلال الشبكية السُّكَّرِيّ and مرض كلوي في المرحلة النهائية further underscores the shared microvascular pathology that links diabetic eye disease and diabetic kidney disease, highlighting the importance of early detection and integrated management of these complications in patients with any form of diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 2 (disorder), Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 are all specific forms of diabetes that map directly to مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus). The condition described as “diabetes or abnormal glucose tolerance complicating pregnancy; childbirth; or the puerperium” is also a manifestation of مرض السكري, representing gestational or pregnancy‑related glucose intolerance within the broader diabetes spectrum. Norgestrel is contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, indicating that this emergency contraceptive should be avoided in individuals with diabetes. Retinal disorder (اضطراب في الشبكية) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, reflecting the well‑known risk of diabetic retinopathy in diabetic patients. Finally, malfunction of the human INSR protein (INSR protein, human) is linked to the development of مرض السكري, underscoring the role of insulin‑receptor defects in the pathogenesis of diabetes. Together, these relationships illustrate the diverse clinical presentations, contraindications, complications, and molecular mechanisms associated with diabetes mellitus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1134
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري) is linked to several clinical and genetic contexts. It is clinically associated with toe amputation (بتر أصبع القدم) and retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية), reflecting the severe vascular complications that can arise in uncontrolled disease. Specific forms of neonatal diabetes, including Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 and Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 2 (DIABETES MELLITUS, TRANSIENT NEONATAL, 1 and 2), as well as the syndrome described as Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes, are all mapped to the broader category of diabetes mellitus, indicating that these early‑onset variants are considered subtypes of the same metabolic disorder. Hormonal contraceptives desogestrel and norgestrel are contraindicated in patients with diabetes, underscoring the need for careful medication selection in this population. Metabolic syndrome (متلازمة الأيض) is recorded as having diabetes as a child condition, illustrating that diabetes is a core component of the syndrome’s cluster of risk factors. The comorbidity concept (komorbidita) is connected to both diabetes and metabolic syndrome, highlighting the frequent co‑occurrence of diabetes with other metabolic and cardiovascular conditions. An additional, unspecified entity is noted to have a comorbidity value of komorbidita, though the exact nature of this relationship remains unclear. Together, these relationships emphasize the multifaceted nature of diabetes, its complications, its genetic forms, medication considerations, and its integral role within broader metabolic disease clusters.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2895
retrieved_docs.content: Developmental Delay, Epilepsy, and Neonatal Diabetes is identified as being mapped to مرض السكري (diabetes mellitus), indicating that this combination of neurodevelopmental and metabolic abnormalities is considered a manifestation or subtype of diabetes. Similarly, Diabetes Mellitus, Transient Neonatal, 1 is also mapped to مرض السكري, reflecting that transient neonatal diabetes is classified within the broader spectrum of diabetes mellitus. The hormonal contraceptive desogestrel and the medication ethynodioldiacetát are both contraindicated in patients with مرض السكري, highlighting the need to avoid these drugs in diabetic individuals due to potential adverse effects or reduced safety. Additionally, اضطراب في الشبكية (retinal disorder) is clinically associated with مرض السكري, underscoring the well‑known relationship between diabetes and retinal complications such as diabetic retinopathy. Together, these connections emphasize that various neonatal and developmental forms of diabetes, specific pharmacologic contraindications, and retinal pathology are all interrelated aspects of the clinical picture of diabetes mellitus, informing both diagnosis and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 360
retrieved_docs.content: Amputation of the lower extremity is a hierarchical surgical category that includes specific procedures such as amputation of a toe (بتر أصبع القدم), below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), and above‑knee amputation (بتر فوق الركبة). All of these limb‑loss procedures are clinically associated with diabetes mellitus (مرض السكري), reflecting the strong link between chronic hyperglycemia and peripheral vascular disease that often necessitates amputation. In addition to limb complications, diabetes mellitus is also clinically associated with retinal disorders (اضطراب في الشبكية), underscoring the disease’s impact on ocular health. Genetic and molecular factors further connect to diabetes: malfunction of the human insulin receptor protein (INSR protein, human) is linked to the development of diabetes, highlighting the role of insulin signaling defects in disease pathogenesis.
